draft.introduction: The Wisdom of Solomon is a Jewish text composed in Greek, almost certainly in Alexandria, probably in the first century BCE. It is attributed to King Solomon but is actually a product of the Hellenistic Jewish diaspora, written by an author deeply immersed in both Jewish Scripture and Greek philosophy. The book is canonical in Catholic and Orthodox traditions and appears in the Septuagint, though it was excluded from the later rabbinic canon. Chapter 7, verse 22 through chapter 8, verse 1 contains the most sustained description of divine Wisdom (Sophia) in Jewish literature. Wisdom is described as a “breath of the power of God, a pure emanation of the glory of the Almighty,” a “reflection of eternal light” and an “image of his goodness.” This language of emanation—Wisdom flowing from God as light flows from the sun, without diminishing the source—became foundational for both Christian theology (where it was applied to the Son and the Holy Spirit) and Gnostic cosmology (where it described the procession of aeons from the divine Pleroma). The passage stands at the headwaters of the entire theosis tradition.

translation.text: For in Wisdom there is a spirit that is intelligent, holy, unique, manifold, subtle, mobile, clear, unpolluted, distinct, invulnerable, loving the good, keen, irresistible, beneficent, humane, steadfast, sure, free from anxiety, all-powerful, overseeing all, and penetrating through all spirits that are intelligent, pure, and most subtle.
translation.original_text: ἔστιν γὰρ ἐν αὐτῇ πνεῦμα νοερόν, ἅγιον, μονογενές, πολυμερές, λεπτόν, εὐκίνητον, τρανόν

translation.text: For Wisdom is more mobile than any motion; because of her pureness she pervades and penetrates all things.

For she is a breath of the power of God, and a pure emanation of the glory of the Almighty; therefore nothing defiled gains entrance into her. For she is a reflection of eternal light, a spotless mirror of the working of God, and an image of his goodness.
translation.original_text: ἀτμὶς γάρ ἐστιν τῆς τοῦ θεοῦ δυνάμεως καὶ ἀπόρροια τῆς τοῦ παντοκράτορος δόξης εἰλικρινής

translation.text: Although she is but one, she can do all things, and while remaining in herself, she renews all things; in every generation she passes into holy souls and makes them friends of God, and prophets.
translation.original_text: μία δὲ οὖσα πάντα δύναται καὶ μένουσα ἐν αὑτῇ τὰ πάντα καινίζει

translation.text: For God loves nothing so much as the person who lives with Wisdom. For she is more beautiful than the sun, and excels every constellation of the stars. Compared with the light, she is found to be superior.
translation.original_text: οὐδὲν γὰρ ἀγαπᾷ ὁ θεὸς εἰ μὴ τὸν σοφίᾳ συνοικοῦντα

translation.text: For against Wisdom evil does not prevail. She reaches mightily from one end of the earth to the other, and she orders all things well.
translation.original_text: σοφίας δὲ οὐ κατισχύει κακία. διατείνει δὲ ἀπὸ πέρατος εἰς πέρας εὐρώστως

translator_notes.note: The twenty-one attributes of Wisdom’s spirit (seven times three, both sacred numbers) echo Stoic descriptions of the pneuma that pervades all things. The author of Wisdom is deliberately translating Jewish Sophia theology into the language of Greek philosophy. The term monogenes (“unique”) here applied to Wisdom’s spirit is the same word later applied to Christ in John 1:14.

The Greek aporroia (“emanation”) is the key term. It implies that Wisdom flows from God as fragrance flows from a substance—distinct from the source, yet sharing its essence. This concept of divine emanation became the foundation of Neoplatonic theology, Gnostic cosmology, and the Christian doctrine of the procession of the Son from the Father. Hebrews 1:3 (“the radiance of God’s glory”) directly echoes this passage.

The statement that Wisdom “passes into holy souls and makes them friends of God, and prophets” is a direct theosis claim within the Jewish wisdom tradition. It asserts that the divine Wisdom enters individual human beings, transforming them into something more than they were. The phrase “friends of God” (philous theou) echoes the designation of Abraham in Isaiah 41:8 and anticipates the language of John 15:15.
